content.problemStatement:
Roger loves drawing polygons. For an arbitrary sequence of points in a 2D plane P0,P1,…,PM−1P_0, P_1, \ldots, P_{M-1}P0​,P1​,…,PM−1​, he can draw a polygon with MMM sides by connecting the points PiP_iPi​ and P(i+1)%MP_{(i+1)\%M}P(i+1)%M​ with a straight line segment for each valid iii. As a result, any point forms a polygon with M=1M = 1M=1, any two distinct points also form a polygon with M=2M = 2M=2 and a polygon may have sides with length 000.

Roger calls a multiset SSS of MMM integers polygonable if there is a polygon created from a sequence with size MMM (as defined above) such that the multiset of its side lengths is equal to SSS. For example, the multisets (0,0)(0, 0)(0,0), (1,3,3)(1, 3, 3)(1,3,3) or (9,9)(9, 9)(9,9) are all polygonable, but (4)(4)(4) and (2,5,8)(2, 5, 8)(2,5,8) are not.

Roger has a tree with NNN vertices (numbered 111 through NNN), where each edge is coloured in one of KKK colours (numbered 111 through NNN). He wants to pick an unordered pair of distinct vertices in the tree and create a multiset S=(S1,S2,…,SK)S = (S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_K)S=(S1​,S2​,…,SK​), where SiS_iSi​ is the number of edges on the path between the chosen vertices which have colour iii. Now, Roger is wondering: in how many ways can he pick a pair of vertices such that the resulting multiset SSS is polygonable? Find the number of such pairs.
constraints: the graph described on the input is a tree
the sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 2⋅1052 \cdot 10^52⋅105
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and KKK.
Each of the next N−1N-1N−1 lines contains three space-separated integers vvv, uuu and ccc, denoting an edge with colour ccc between vertices vvv and uuu.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer ― the number of valid pairs.
